program: Travel English -- Europe Without a Translator
hub_subtitle: Travel English for the airport, the check-in counter, the hotel and passport control — so that Europe needs no translator

lesson.menu_title: Let Me Explain -- Planning the Trip Her Own Way
lesson.menu_desc: Gerund and infinitive for plans and preferences: enjoy walking, prefer taking the train, want to rent a car. A travel desk, an agent with a standard all-inclusive package -- and, for the first time, a woman who says "let me explain" and plans her own trip, in English, with nobody translating for her -- 37 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 14 -- Let Me Explain
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rosângela | Lesson 14 -- Let Me Explain
lesson.grammar_point: gerund vs infinitive (verb + -ing after like/love/enjoy/prefer/avoid; verb + to after want/need/decide/plan/hope)

listenings.text: Let me tell you a secret about my job. I am a travel agent, and I have two kinds of client. The first kind sits down and says: just book me something. A package. Everything decided. The flights, the hotel, the tours, the meals, all together for one price. And I do it, and it is fine, and they go, and half of them come back a little sad, because they spent two weeks on a bus with fifty strangers, doing things they do not enjoy. They saw the big landmarks and they never saw a single small street. The second kind is different. The second kind sits down and says: let me explain. I prefer trains to planes. I enjoy walking. I avoid big tours. I want to rent a car for one day. I need flexible dates. And that person -- that person always gets the trip they wanted, because they told me what it was. Here is the thing nobody believes: the second kind is not the one with the best English. Some of them have terrible English. But they know three little verbs -- I like, I prefer, I want -- and they are not afraid to use them. So please, when you sit down with me, do not say just book me anything. Tell me what you love doing, and what you cannot stand. Say let me explain. It is not a bother. It is my favorite sentence in the world, because it is the moment the trip becomes yours instead of mine.

My name is Teresa, I am seventy-four, and for most of my life other people planned my trips. My husband booked everything. Then my daughter booked everything. They chose the hotels, they chose the tours, they chose the restaurants, and I said yes to all of it. Yes, yes, yes. I went to beautiful places and I did things I did not enjoy, because it was easier to say yes than to explain. And do you know what changed it? Not a course. Not a grammar book. Three verbs. One afternoon, in a small office in Lisbon, an agent asked me what I wanted, and instead of saying yes, I said: let me explain. I love walking. I prefer small hotels to big ones. I avoid guided tours, and I do not want an all-inclusive package -- I like choosing my own dinner. He wrote it all down, and he built me the trip I had wanted for forty years and never asked for. I cried a little in that office, and he pretended not to see. So this is what I want to tell you, especially if you are a woman my age who has always let somebody else decide. You do not need perfect English to say what you like. You need three verbs and a little courage. I like. I prefer. I want. Learn to finish those three sentences, and no travel agent, in any country, will ever choose your life for you again.

structure.title: Two lists of verbs, and one to learn by heart
cards: like, love, enjoy, prefer, avoid, don't mind
After these, the second verb wears -ing. "I enjoy walking." "I love traveling by train." "I avoid taking big tours." "I don't mind waiting." Never "I enjoy to walk" -- these verbs do not take to.
want, need, decide, plan, hope, would like

After these, the second verb takes to. "I want to rent a car." "I have decided to travel light." "I hope to see the coast." "I would like to pay only the deposit." Never "I want going" -- these verbs take to, not -ing.
prefer A to B (learn it by heart)

"I prefer trains to planes." With two actions, both wear -ing and a little to sits in the middle: "I prefer taking the train to flying." One preference, two -ing verbs, one to. That is the whole shape.

paras: It is ten in the morning, and Rosângela is sitting at a small travel agent's desk near her hotel, planning the second week of the trip. Her husband is on a chair by the door, quiet, with a coffee -- he does not speak English, and this morning he does not need to. The agent, a young man called Miguel, slides a shiny brochure across the desk and taps the first page. "This is our most popular one," he says. "An all-inclusive package. Everything is decided for you -- the hotel, the meals, a big guided tour every morning on the bus. You pay a deposit today, and you don't think about anything again." And Rosângela, who for forty years let somebody else decide, puts her hand gently on the brochure, and says three words she has never said in English before: "Let me explain."

"I don't want an all-inclusive package," she says, slowly, one piece at a time. "I enjoy choosing my own dinner. And I prefer taking the train to flying -- I love watching the countryside go by. I avoid big guided tours; I like walking the small streets on my own, with my husband. But I would like to rent a car for one day, for a day trip to the coast -- the little towns off the main road. My dates are flexible: I need to be able to change them. And for the flight home, I want a nonstop one -- I don't enjoy waiting eight hours in an airport for a layover." Miguel stops writing and looks up. "That," he says, "is the clearest list I have had all month. Most people just say: book me anything."

Here is what Miguel does not know. Four years ago, in Paris, her son sat in this same kind of chair and did all the talking, and Rosângela nodded along to a trip he chose. She was grateful, and his English was perfect, and that was never the problem. The problem is that a trip you did not ask for is a trip that belongs to somebody else. This morning, in a small office with a coffee going cold beside her, Rosângela did not nod. She explained. She used two little shapes -- a verb with -ing for the things she loves, a verb with to for the things she plans -- and with them she built her own second week, out loud, herself. It is not the grammar that is hard. It is saying, at seventy, for the first time: this is what I like, and this is what I want.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Miguel convinces Rosângela that the all-inclusive package is the best and easiest choice, so she pays the deposit and lets him decide everything.
Instead of taking the package everyone takes, Rosângela explains what she actually prefers -- and, for the first time, she plans her own trip, in English, with nobody translating for her.
Rosângela cannot decide what she wants, so she asks Miguel and her husband to choose the hotel, the tours and the meals for her.

items: Rosângela takes the all-inclusive package that Miguel offers her first.
The opposite: she puts her hand on the brochure and says "Let me explain." She says clearly, "I don't want an all-inclusive package -- I enjoy choosing my own dinner."
Rosângela prefers taking the train to flying, and she enjoys walking the small streets on her own.
She says it herself: "I prefer taking the train to flying... I like walking the small streets on my own, with my husband."
She would like to rent a car for one day, to make a day trip to the coast.
"I would like to rent a car for one day, for a day trip to the coast -- the little towns off the main road."
For years Rosângela planned every trip herself, and her son only carried the bags.
The opposite: four years ago in Paris her son did all the talking and chose the trip, and she nodded along. Tonight, for the first time, she explained her own trip, in English.
Miguel says most of his clients tell him exactly what they like and how they want to travel.
He says most people just say "book me anything." Rosângela's list is the clearest he has had all month -- and English is not even her language.

items.situation: The agent offers you the all-inclusive package, with every meal and tour decided for you. Say, politely, that you prefer to choose your own restaurants.
tips: Thank you, but I don't want an all-inclusive package. I prefer choosing my own restaurants.
No, thank you. I enjoy choosing where I eat.

items.situation: He asks how you like to travel between cities. You love the train, and you don't like flying. Tell him.
tips: I love traveling by train. I don't enjoy flying.
I prefer taking the train to flying -- I like watching the countryside.

items.situation: He suggests a big guided tour on a bus every morning. You want to walk the small streets on your own. Say what you prefer.
tips: I'd rather walk on my own. I avoid big guided tours.
I prefer walking the small streets to taking a bus tour.

items.situation: You want a car for one day, to see the coast. Ask him about renting one.
tips: I would like to rent a car for a day trip to the coast.
Can I rent a car for one day? I want to see the little towns on the coast.

items.situation: He asks for your exact dates. You are not sure yet -- you need to be able to change them. Tell him.
tips: My dates are flexible. I need to be able to change them.
I'm not sure yet -- I would like flexible dates, please.

items.situation: He offers a cheaper flight with a long layover, or a nonstop one that costs more. Say which you prefer, and why.
tips: I prefer a nonstop flight to a long layover. I don't enjoy waiting in airports.
I'd rather pay more for a nonstop flight. I avoid long layovers.

items.situation: He needs a deposit to hold the booking, but you want to decide the rest tomorrow. Ask to pay only the deposit now.
tips: Can I pay only the deposit now and decide the rest tomorrow?
I would like to pay the deposit today, and finish booking tomorrow.

items.situation: He hands you a brochure and asks what you enjoy doing on a trip, so he can plan the right one. Tell him.
tips: I enjoy walking, visiting small towns, and eating in the squares. I avoid crowds.
I love exploring on foot. I don't enjoy shopping or big tours.
